pattern: A subarray can be zeroed out this way exactly when every bit position appears an even number of times within it, which is the same condition as the XOR of the whole subarray being zero, so counting beautiful subarrays becomes counting subarrays whose XOR vanishes.
hints: The operation only ever cancels one matching set bit from two elements at a time, so a subarray can reach all zeros exactly when, at every single bit position, the count of elements having that bit set is even.
That even-count-at-every-bit condition is precisely what it means for the XOR of the whole subarray to equal zero, so the problem becomes: count subarrays whose XOR is zero.

Track a running prefix XOR (starting at zero before the array begins) and a count of how many times each prefix XOR value has been seen so far; a subarray's XOR is zero exactly when its two boundary prefix values match, so add the running count for the current prefix value before recording it.

edge_inputs.description: all zeros, every subarray is trivially beautiful
single non-zero element, never beautiful on its own
a matching pair whose xor cancels, flanking an unrelated value
three identical non-zero values, only the full-length even-multiple grouping is beautiful
values needing several different bits to be cancelled at once
the largest allowed values, repeated so the whole array cancels
no subarray at all is beautiful

stress.description: large random arrays across the full value range

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def beautifulSubarrays(self, nums):
        from collections import defaultdict
        cnt = defaultdict(int)
        cnt[0] = 1
        prefix = 0
        total = 0
        for v in nums:
            prefix ^= v
            total += cnt[prefix]
            cnt[prefix] += 1
        return total
